name: IBM Watson Health
summary: IBM's flagship AI-in-healthcare initiative aimed to revolutionize cancer diagnosis and treatment recommendations using Watson's cognitive computing, partnering with MD Anderson and other hospitals, but the technology failed to deliver reliable, clinically useful oncology recommendations at scale. IBM sold off Watson Health assets to Francisco Partners in 2022, which rebranded them as Merative.
lesson: Marketing AI capabilities years ahead of what the underlying technology could reliably deliver in high-stakes clinical settings erodes trust and invites failure.
source: STAT News and Wall Street Journal investigations (2017-2021); IBM divestiture announcement (2022)

name: Enlitic
summary: One of the earliest deep-learning radiology startups, Enlitic pioneered AI for reading X-rays and CT scans but struggled for years to gain regulatory clearance and hospital adoption, going through repeated leadership changes, layoffs, and pivots before fading into near-total obscurity.
lesson: Being first to market with a bold AI vision in medicine doesn't guarantee success if regulatory pathways and clinical workflow integration aren't solved early.
source: TechCrunch and Forbes coverage of early medical AI pioneers

name: Zebra Medical Vision
summary: Israeli radiology AI company built a broad suite of FDA-cleared imaging algorithms but could not build a sustainable standalone business model and was acquired by imaging hardware startup Nanox in a distressed all-stock deal at a fraction of its peak valuation.
lesson: A broad portfolio of point AI algorithms sold piecemeal to hospitals often can't generate enough revenue to justify continued independent operation.
source: Calcalist and TechCrunch coverage of the Nanox-Zebra acquisition (2021)

name: Babylon Health
summary: UK-based AI symptom-checker and virtual GP platform expanded aggressively via a NYSE SPAC listing and US value-based care contracts, but its AI triage tool faced clinical safety criticism and the company collapsed under debt and unprofitable US contracts, filing for insolvency in 2023.
lesson: Consumer-facing diagnostic AI claims must withstand independent clinical scrutiny, and value-based care expansion can mask deep unit-economics problems until it's too late.
source: Financial Times and CNBC coverage of Babylon Health's 2023 collapse

name: Pear Therapeutics
summary: Pioneer of FDA-cleared prescription digital therapeutics, including AI-driven behavioral algorithms for substance use and insomnia, Pear could not get insurers to reimburse its products at scale, burning through its funding before filing for Chapter 11 bankruptcy and selling off assets.
lesson: FDA clearance for an AI-enabled therapeutic is meaningless without a reimbursement pathway that pays for the ongoing cost of delivering it.
source: STAT News and Fierce Healthcare bankruptcy coverage (2023)

name: Olive AI
summary: Olive raised nearly $900M promising an 'AI workforce' to automate healthcare administrative and clinical-adjacent tasks, but overpromised results, churned customers, and ultimately shut down after selling its remaining business units piecemeal.
lesson: Rapid fundraising at inflated valuations can mask a widening gap between marketed AI capability and delivered customer value.
source: Forbes and STAT News investigation into Olive AI (2023)

name: Forward (Forward Health)
summary: Backed by Adrian Aoun and large venture rounds, Forward built AI-powered primary care clinics and later self-serve health 'CarePods,' but could never make the capital-intensive physical clinic model profitable and abruptly ceased all operations in 2025.
lesson: Physical, hardware-heavy healthcare delivery models require a much longer and costlier path to unit economics than most software-only AI startups.
source: TechCrunch and Fierce Healthcare shutdown coverage (2025)

name: uBiome
summary: Microbiome-sequencing startup marketed its algorithm-driven gut-health tests direct to consumers and physicians, but investigators found it billed insurers for medically unnecessary tests and misrepresented its science, leading to an FBI raid, federal fraud charges against founders, and bankruptcy.
lesson: Growth built on aggressive insurance billing practices for unproven diagnostic algorithms invites regulatory and criminal scrutiny that can destroy a company overnight.
source: Wall Street Journal reporting and SEC fraud charges against uBiome founders (2021)

name: BenevolentAI
summary: Once Europe's most-hyped AI drug discovery unicorn, BenevolentAI's platform failed to produce late-stage clinical wins, its lead dermatitis drug missed trial endpoints, and its public listing lost over 90% of its value before a rescue financing and effective take-private restructuring in 2023-2024.
lesson: AI-generated drug candidates still must clear the same expensive, high-failure-rate clinical trial gauntlet as any other drug — platform hype does not de-risk biology.
source: Financial Times and Reuters coverage of BenevolentAI's 2023-24 collapse

name: Berg Health (Berg Pharma)
summary: Berg pioneered 'Interrogative Biology,' using AI to mine patient biological data for drug and diagnostic targets in cancer and Parkinson's disease, but after over a decade and hundreds of millions invested it never brought a product to market and quietly faded from industry relevance.
lesson: A novel AI-driven scientific methodology needs interim, verifiable wins — not just a compelling narrative — to sustain investor confidence over a decade-plus R&D timeline.
source: Forbes and Xconomy coverage of Berg's AI drug discovery ambitions

name: Human Longevity Inc
summary: Founded to build the world's largest genomic and health database paired with AI-driven diagnostics for early disease detection, HLI was plagued by founder conflicts between Craig Venter and investors, mass layoffs, and failed to commercialize its whole-body scanning and genomics products.
lesson: Even world-renowned scientific founders can sink a company when governance conflicts distract from execution on a genuinely hard AI-diagnostics business model.
source: STAT News and San Diego Union-Tribune coverage of HLI leadership turmoil

name: Sema4 (GeneDx Holdings)
summary: Sema4 went public via SPAC promising an AI-driven 'health intelligence' platform combining genomic and clinical data, but burned cash rapidly on unprofitable testing operations, cut most of its workforce, and sold off its core diagnostics business in 2023 to survive as a much smaller renamed entity.
lesson: SPAC-fueled growth targets for data-driven diagnostics businesses can outrun the actual reimbursement and margin economics of clinical testing.
source: Fierce Healthcare and MedCity News coverage of Sema4's 2022-23 restructuring

name: Cue Health
summary: Cue Health's AI-connected molecular diagnostics platform, including a COVID test with a companion app, scaled massively during the pandemic on federal contracts, but demand collapsed post-pandemic and the company could not build a sustainable non-COVID diagnostics business, ceasing most operations and delisting from Nasdaq in 2024.
lesson: Building a diagnostics company primarily around a single pandemic-driven demand spike leaves almost no margin for error when that demand evaporates.
source: CNBC and MedTech Dive coverage of Cue Health's 2024 wind-down

name: Your.MD
summary: UK-based AI symptom-checker app aimed to be a 'personal health assistant' for millions of users but failed to convert a large free user base into a viable B2B2C or enterprise licensing revenue model before running out of funding and shutting down.
lesson: A large consumer user base for a free AI health tool is not, by itself, a business model — monetization must be proven long before the runway ends.
source: MobiHealthNews and TechCrunch coverage of Your.MD's shutdown (2021)

name: HealthTap
summary: One of the earliest AI symptom-checker and virtual-doctor platforms, HealthTap raised significant funding and attracted millions of users, but repeated pivots between consumer, enterprise, and telehealth models never produced durable revenue, and the company faded from prominence.
lesson: Repeatedly pivoting a consumer health AI product between business models without settling on one durable value proposition erodes both team focus and investor patience.
source: TechCrunch and CNBC early coverage of HealthTap's pivots

name: Sensyne Health
summary: UK AI clinical data analytics company, backed by NHS data-sharing partnerships and co-founded by a former UK government minister, went public on the London Stock Exchange but never achieved profitability and collapsed into insolvency after burning through its cash reserves.
lesson: Access to exclusive public health data partnerships is a moat only if it can be converted into a commercially viable, repeatable product — access alone doesn't pay the bills.
source: Financial Times and Sky News coverage of Sensyne Health's 2023 collapse

name: Analytics 4 Life (Cortex/CorVista)
summary: Developed a noninvasive AI-based cardiac imaging technology (CorVista) meant to replace costlier invasive coronary tests, but repeated delays in FDA clearance and clinical validation drained investor patience and capital before achieving wide commercial launch.
lesson: Regulatory delays for novel diagnostic AI devices compound cash-burn risk, especially when the go-to-market plan assumed a much faster clearance timeline.
source: MedTech Dive and trade press coverage of A4L/CorVista funding rounds

name: Woebot Health
summary: Built a clinically-studied AI chatbot for mental health support, but as generative-AI chatbots built on large language models became ubiquitous and free, Woebot's differentiated, clinically validated approach could not compete on cost or reach, leading it to shut down its consumer app.
lesson: A clinically rigorous but narrow AI product can be commoditized overnight when general-purpose foundation models offer a 'good enough' alternative for free.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Woebot Health's app shutdown (2025)

name: K Health
summary: K Health built an AI-driven symptom checker and virtual primary care app using anonymized patient data, but after failing to build a profitable direct-to-consumer subscription business, it underwent major layoffs and refocused on white-labeled AI for health systems and payers.
lesson: Consumer AI health apps face brutal customer acquisition costs; pivoting to B2B distribution is often the only viable path, but doing so late can cost most of the original team and momentum.
source: Fierce Healthcare and Forbes coverage of K Health's 2023-24 restructuring

name: Valo Health
summary: Backed by Flagship Pioneering, Valo Health combined human clinical data with an 'in silico' AI drug discovery platform (Opal), but after failing to translate the platform hype into near-term clinical pipeline wins, it cut a significant share of its workforce amid a tightening AI-biotech funding environment.
lesson: The multi-year, multi-hundred-million-dollar cost of proving an AI drug discovery platform works leaves little room for a downturn in venture funding before the science pays off.
source: Endpoints News and Fierce Biotech coverage of Valo Health layoffs (2024)

name: twoXAR
summary: Early AI drug discovery startup used machine learning to identify existing drugs for new disease indications, but its low-capital, licensing-heavy business model could not generate enough revenue or partnership traction to sustain the company, leading to a quiet shutdown.
lesson: An AI drug-repurposing business model that depends on convincing pharma partners to license predictions rather than owning assets outright often struggles to capture enough value to be venture-scale.
source: Crunchbase and industry trade coverage of twoXAR's 2022 closure

name: Cyclica
summary: Toronto-based AI drug discovery company built a polypharmacology-prediction platform, but after years without a major independent pipeline win, was acquired by larger rival Recursion Pharmaceuticals for a modest, largely stock-based sum in a distressed deal.
lesson: In AI drug discovery, scale and proprietary data volume increasingly determine winners — smaller platforms without differentiated data often end up as acquisition targets rather than independent successes.
source: Endpoints News and Recursion Pharmaceuticals acquisition announcement (2023)

name: Numerate
summary: One of the earliest AI-driven small-molecule drug discovery companies, Numerate partnered with pharma on several disease programs but never achieved a major independent drug approval or acquisition, gradually scaling down and fading from the industry around 2019-2020.
lesson: Being early to an AI drug discovery thesis is not enough — without a breakthrough clinical result or major partnership renewal, quiet attrition is the default outcome.
source: Xconomy and industry archives on early AI drug discovery startups

name: Arterys
summary: Arterys built one of the first FDA-cleared cloud-based deep-learning platforms for cardiac and oncology imaging analysis, but struggled to convert its regulatory firsts into a scalable, profitable hospital sales motion before fading from the competitive radiology-AI landscape.
lesson: Being first to FDA clearance in medical imaging AI provides a headline, not a moat — a crowded field of well-funded competitors can still out-execute on sales and integration.
source: Radiology Business and industry coverage of early cloud imaging-AI platforms

name: Butterfly Network
summary: Butterfly Network's handheld, AI-guided ultrasound-on-a-chip promised to democratize imaging diagnostics, but after going public via SPAC at a rich valuation, slower-than-expected adoption and heavy cash burn forced major layoffs and a collapse of over 90% in market value.
lesson: Hardware-plus-AI medical devices carry the combined capital intensity of both device manufacturing and software development, which SPAC-era valuations often failed to price in realistically.
source: MedTech Dive and CNBC coverage of Butterfly Network's post-SPAC struggles

name: MedyMatch Technology
summary: Israeli startup developed AI to detect brain hemorrhages and strokes from CT scans in the ER, partnering closely with Samsung, but never scaled beyond a handful of deployments and gradually receded from the market as better-funded rivals like Viz.ai and Aidoc captured the stroke-AI category.
lesson: In a fast-moving diagnostic AI niche, being early with a single strategic partner is not enough if faster-scaling, more broadly funded competitors define the category.
source: Calcalist and MobiHealthNews coverage of the MedyMatch-Samsung partnership

name: AiCure
summary: Pioneered AI-powered computer vision to confirm medication ingestion for clinical trials and chronic-disease patients, but the niche market for ingestion-verification technology proved too small to sustain venture-scale growth, and the company faded from visibility.
lesson: A genuinely novel AI application can still fail if the total addressable market for that specific use case is narrower than the venture funding raised implies it needs to be.
source: MobiHealthNews and clinical trial technology trade press coverage

name: Nuritas
summary: Irish AI company used machine learning to discover bioactive peptides from natural sources for pharma, nutrition, and skincare applications, but after ambitious hiring and a slower-than-expected path to commercial peptide royalties, was forced into deep layoffs during the 2023 biotech funding downturn.
lesson: AI-discovery companies that sell into multiple end-markets (pharma, consumer, agri) risk spreading thin the capital and focus needed to prove out any single one commercially.
source: Irish Times and Endpoints News coverage of Nuritas layoffs (2023)

name: Kheiron Medical Technologies
summary: UK-based breast-cancer screening AI company (Mia) achieved strong clinical validation and NHS pilot deployments, but converting pilots into large-scale, reimbursed national screening contracts proved much slower than projected, straining its funding runway and forcing restructuring.
lesson: Strong clinical validation data does not guarantee fast procurement — national health system sales cycles can outlast a startup's funding runway even for genuinely effective AI.
source: Sifted and MedTech Dive coverage of Kheiron Medical's funding and NHS deployments

name: CloudMedX
summary: Early clinical-NLP and AI risk-prediction startup aimed to help hospitals reduce readmissions and improve documentation, but struggled to raise beyond a modest seed round in a market being consolidated by larger, better-capitalized health-AI platforms, eventually shutting down quietly.
lesson: Undercapitalized clinical-AI startups competing against well-funded platform players in the same niche rarely survive a prolonged enterprise sales cycle.
source: Crunchbase and health-IT trade press coverage of CloudMedX's closure

name: Curai Health
summary: Founded by ex-Google AI researchers to build an AI-driven virtual primary care service that could scale physician capacity, Curai struggled to prove the unit economics of AI-augmented, human-in-the-loop primary care at scale, leading to substantial layoffs and restructuring in 2023-2024.
lesson: Even with elite AI research talent, 'AI-augmented' clinical labor models must still prove that per-visit costs fall enough to beat traditional telehealth economics.
source: Fierce Healthcare and Business Insider coverage of Curai Health layoffs

name: 98point6
summary: Built an AI-assisted asynchronous text-based virtual primary care platform, automating parts of the diagnostic intake process, but after failing to reach profitability as a standalone consumer and enterprise telehealth brand, sold its clinical assets to Transcarent and effectively dissolved.
lesson: AI-assisted care-delivery startups competing in the crowded telehealth market need a genuinely differentiated cost structure, not just a novel interface, to survive consolidation.
source: GeekWire and Fierce Healthcare coverage of 98point6's 2023 asset sale

name: Mindstrong Health
summary: Founded by former NIMH director Tom Insel, Mindstrong used smartphone-interaction AI ('digital phenotyping') to detect signs of mental health relapse, but after a strategic pivot away from its original consumer product and mounting cash burn, the company shut down entirely in 2023.
lesson: A scientifically prestigious founding team and a genuinely novel biomarker approach can still fail if the company pivots away from its core product before proving commercial viability.
source: STAT News coverage of Mindstrong Health's 2023 shutdown

name: Owlstone Medical
summary: UK breath-biopsy company combined chemical sensing with AI/machine-learning analysis to detect diseases like cancer non-invasively from exhaled breath, but slower-than-expected clinical validation and reimbursement pathways forced substantial layoffs during a tight funding market in 2023.
lesson: Novel biomarker-plus-AI diagnostics platforms often need far more clinical validation capital and time than initially modeled, especially for a genuinely new sample type like breath.
source: MedTech Dive and Cambridge Independent coverage of Owlstone Medical's 2023 layoffs

name: Genome Medical
summary: Combined AI-assisted genetic risk triage with a nationwide telegenetics counseling network to help health systems and payers scale genomic medicine, but could not build a sustainable reimbursement model for genetic counseling services and ceased operations in 2024.
lesson: Services businesses layered on top of AI-driven risk stratification still need payers to reimburse the human service component, which can be the real bottleneck to scale.
source: Fierce Healthcare and GenomeWeb coverage of Genome Medical's 2024 closure

name: Better Therapeutics
summary: Developed an FDA-cleared prescription digital therapeutic (AspyreRx) using AI-personalized cognitive behavioral therapy to treat cardiometabolic disease, but after going public via SPAC could not achieve meaningful payer reimbursement or revenue before filing for Chapter 11 bankruptcy and delisting from Nasdaq.
lesson: FDA clearance and a SPAC listing create the appearance of maturity, but without payer contracts actually paying for the product, cash burn continues unchecked.
source: MobiHealthNews and Fierce Healthcare coverage of Better Therapeutics' bankruptcy (2023)

name: Quartet Health
summary: Used AI and analytics to match primary care patients with appropriate behavioral health providers and coordinate collaborative care, but after years of subscale growth and rising cash burn, ceased operations in 2023.
lesson: Care-coordination AI platforms depend on deep two-sided integration with both primary care and behavioral health providers — partial adoption on either side undermines the whole value proposition.
source: Behavioral Health Business and Fierce Healthcare coverage of Quartet Health's 2023 shutdown

name: Higi
summary: Deployed a national network of AI-enabled health-screening kiosks in retail pharmacies to capture biometric data and risk-screen users, but advertising- and data-driven revenue never covered the capital cost of maintaining thousands of kiosks, leading to a full shutdown.
lesson: Physical AI-screening infrastructure at national retail scale carries maintenance and real-estate costs that a data-monetization business model rarely covers fast enough.
source: MedCity News and Chicago Tribune coverage of Higi's 2023 closure

name: Onduo (Verily)
summary: Alphabet's Verily launched Onduo as an AI-powered virtual diabetes clinic combining connected devices and coaching, but after failing to scale profitably even with Alphabet's backing, Verily discontinued the service in 2022 and folded remaining capabilities into other product lines.
lesson: Even a well-capitalized corporate parent will shut down an AI health service that can't demonstrate a credible path to standalone unit economics.
source: CNBC and Fierce Healthcare coverage of Verily's Onduo discontinuation (2022)

name: Tempus AI
summary: Built a large-scale precision-medicine platform combining genomic sequencing, clinical data, and AI/ML models to guide oncology treatment decisions, growing into one of the largest AI-health datasets in the world and completing a successful Nasdaq IPO in 2024.
lesson: Building a proprietary, continuously growing multimodal clinical dataset creates a defensible data moat that pure algorithmic startups struggle to replicate.
source: Nasdaq IPO filings and Reuters coverage of Tempus AI's 2024 IPO

name: Viz.ai
summary: Built an FDA-cleared AI platform that automatically flags large-vessel-occlusion strokes on CT scans and alerts specialists in minutes, becoming one of the most widely deployed clinical AI triage tools in US stroke centers and expanding into cardiology and other specialties.
lesson: Focusing AI on a single, measurable, time-critical clinical workflow (faster stroke treatment) makes the value proposition unambiguous to both clinicians and payers.
source: Fierce Healthcare and company funding announcements

name: Aidoc
summary: Israeli radiology-AI company built a platform of FDA-cleared algorithms that flag critical findings such as strokes, pulmonary embolisms, and fractures across CT scans, becoming one of the most widely deployed AI triage suites in US and global hospital radiology departments.
lesson: Expanding a single successful triage algorithm into a broader platform of clinical-workflow-integrated tools compounds the value of each hospital relationship.
source: Fierce Healthcare and Aidoc funding round press coverage

name: PathAI
summary: Developed AI-powered pathology software to improve accuracy and speed of tissue-based diagnosis, forming deep partnerships with pharma companies for clinical trial biomarker analysis alongside diagnostic tools for pathologists, sustaining strong growth and funding.
lesson: Pairing a clinical diagnostics product with a lucrative pharma biomarker/clinical-trials services business diversifies revenue beyond slow hospital sales cycles.
source: Fierce Biotech and PathAI funding round coverage

name: Recursion Pharmaceuticals
summary: Combined automated cell-biology experimentation with machine learning to industrialize drug discovery, went public on Nasdaq in 2021, and in 2024 merged with fellow AI-drug-discovery company Exscientia to build one of the largest public AI-biotech platforms.
lesson: Owning the entire experimental data-generation pipeline, not just modeling on third-party data, gives an AI drug discovery company a defensible, proprietary data advantage.
source: Nasdaq IPO filings and Recursion-Exscientia merger press release (2024)

name: Insilico Medicine
summary: Hong Kong/US-based AI drug discovery company built an end-to-end generative-AI platform (PandaOmics, Chemistry42) that has advanced multiple AI-designed drug candidates into human clinical trials, including a novel idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis drug, and is pursuing a Hong Kong IPO.
lesson: Demonstrating that an AI-discovered molecule can actually reach and progress through human clinical trials is the single most credibility-building milestone for an AI drug discovery company.
source: Nature Biotechnology reporting and company clinical trial announcements

name: Atomwise
summary: One of the longest-running AI drug discovery companies, Atomwise uses deep learning (AtomNet) to predict small-molecule binding for drug targets, sustaining a long list of pharma partnerships and internal pipeline programs over more than a decade.
lesson: A diversified partnership-plus-internal-pipeline model can sustain an AI drug discovery company through multiple funding cycles even without a blockbuster approved drug yet.
source: Fierce Biotech and Atomwise partnership press releases

name: Owkin
summary: French-American startup combines federated learning, allowing hospitals to collaboratively train AI models without sharing raw patient data, with AI-driven biomarker discovery for oncology, building deep partnerships with pharma companies and academic medical centers.
lesson: Federated learning lets an AI-health startup access sensitive multi-institutional data at scale without the legal and trust barriers of centralizing patient data.
source: Fierce Biotech and Owkin funding round coverage

name: Deep Genomics
summary: Toronto-based AI drug discovery company uses machine learning to predict how genetic variants affect RNA biology, advancing its own pipeline of RNA-targeted therapeutics for genetic diseases into clinical development.
lesson: Focusing AI drug discovery on a well-defined biological mechanism, such as RNA splicing, rather than trying to be a general-purpose platform helps build defensible scientific expertise.
source: Fierce Biotech and Deep Genomics funding announcements

name: XtalPi
summary: China-based AI and robotics drug discovery company combining quantum physics, AI, and automated experimentation for molecular design, completed a successful Hong Kong Stock Exchange IPO in 2024 backed by major pharma partnerships including Eli Lilly and Pfizer.
lesson: Pairing AI prediction with in-house automated 'self-driving lab' experimentation closes the feedback loop that pure computational drug discovery platforms often lack.
source: Reuters reporting and Hong Kong Stock Exchange IPO filings (2024)

name: Paige.AI
summary: Spun out of Memorial Sloan Kettering, Paige built the first FDA-authorized AI software for pathology (prostate cancer detection), leveraging one of the world's largest annotated pathology slide datasets to expand into multiple cancer types.
lesson: An exclusive license to a world-class clinical dataset from a top academic medical center can be the deciding factor in winning the race to regulatory-cleared diagnostic AI.
source: FDA authorization announcement (2021) and Fierce Biotech coverage

name: Ibex Medical Analytics
summary: Israeli AI pathology company built a clinical-grade cancer detection platform deployed across labs in multiple countries, positioning itself as a leading second-read AI tool for pathologists in prostate and breast cancer diagnosis.
lesson: International lab-network deployment and strong peer-reviewed validation studies build the clinical trust needed for pathologists to adopt AI as a second reader.
source: Fierce Biotech and Ibex Medical Analytics funding round coverage

name: Lunit
summary: South Korean radiology and pathology AI company focused on chest X-ray, mammography, and cancer detection achieved a successful IPO on Korea's KOSDAQ exchange and expanded globally, including a partnership and investment relationship with Volpara Health.
lesson: A successful home-market IPO can provide the non-dilutive capital and credibility an AI-diagnostics company needs to fund international expansion.
source: KOSDAQ IPO filings and Lunit press releases (2022)

name: Cleerly
summary: Built an FDA-cleared AI platform that analyzes coronary CT angiograms to quantify plaque and predict heart attack risk, raising large late-stage rounds on strong clinical evidence and growing adoption among cardiologists.
lesson: Targeting a specific, high-mortality diagnostic gap, such as underdiagnosed coronary artery disease, with strong outcomes evidence can attract outsized late-stage capital even in a tighter funding market.
source: Fierce Biotech and Cleerly Series C/D funding announcements

name: Genesis Therapeutics
summary: AI drug discovery company built a proprietary graph neural network platform for molecular design, securing large partnerships with pharma companies including Genentech and raising one of the largest Series rounds in AI biotech.
lesson: Landing a marquee pharma partnership early can validate an AI drug discovery platform's technology and unlock outsized subsequent funding rounds.
source: Fierce Biotech coverage of the Genesis Therapeutics Series C (2023)

name: Iktos
summary: French AI drug design company uses generative AI to design novel small molecules for pharma partners, building a steady base of pharma collaborations, including Merck and Servier, without needing to raise mega-rounds.
lesson: A capital-efficient, partnership-heavy business model can sustain an AI drug discovery company for years without the outsized funding rounds larger competitors require.
source: Fierce Biotech and Iktos partnership announcements

name: Turbine
summary: Hungarian AI drug discovery company built a simulated cell platform to predict cancer drug combination efficacy, securing partnerships with major pharma companies and steady growth funding without major setbacks.
lesson: A differentiated computational approach, such as whole-cell simulation rather than pure pattern-matching machine learning, can carve out a defensible niche in a crowded AI drug discovery field.
source: Fierce Biotech coverage of Turbine's Series B funding

name: Flatiron Health
summary: Built an AI/analytics platform aggregating real-world oncology data from cancer clinics to power clinical decision support and research, and was acquired by Roche for $1.9B in 2018, one of the largest health-data-AI exits to date.
lesson: Aggregating structured real-world clinical data at scale can be more valuable to a strategic acquirer than any single predictive algorithm.
source: Roche acquisition press release (2018) and Fierce Biotech coverage

name: Nuance Communications
summary: Nuance's Dragon Medical and AI-powered clinical documentation and ambient listening tools became deeply embedded in hospital workflows, leading Microsoft to acquire the company for $19.7B in 2021 to anchor its healthcare AI strategy.
lesson: Deep, decades-long workflow integration into clinician documentation habits can make an AI company an irreplaceable acquisition target for a hyperscaler entering healthcare.
source: Microsoft acquisition press release (2021)

name: Caption Health (Bay Labs)
summary: Developed FDA-cleared AI software that guides non-expert clinicians to capture diagnostic-quality cardiac ultrasound images in real time, and was acquired by GE HealthCare in 2023 to integrate into its imaging device portfolio.
lesson: AI that expands who can perform a diagnostic exam, not just who can interpret it, creates a distinct value proposition attractive to imaging-device incumbents.
source: GE HealthCare acquisition press release (2023)

name: Concert AI (Concerto HealthAI)
summary: Built an oncology-focused real-world data and AI platform for clinical decision support, biomarker discovery, and clinical trial matching, forming a major strategic partnership with Merck and other pharma companies.
lesson: Positioning AI-driven real-world evidence generation as essential infrastructure for pharma clinical trials creates recurring, high-value enterprise revenue.
source: Fierce Biotech coverage of the Concert AI-Merck partnership

name: Ada Health
summary: Berlin-based AI symptom-assessment and care-navigation platform serves tens of millions of users globally and pivoted successfully toward B2B licensing to health systems and payers, sustaining steady growth without the collapse seen by rival symptom checkers.
lesson: Symptom-checker AI companies that successfully transition from a free consumer app to licensed B2B/B2B2C distribution can avoid the monetization trap that sank several competitors.
source: MobiHealthNews and Ada Health funding round coverage

name: Suki AI
summary: Built an AI voice assistant for clinical documentation that competes directly with Nuance/Microsoft's Dragon Medical, securing major health system deployments and steady growth funding through the ambient-AI documentation boom.
lesson: Entering an established AI-documentation market as a nimble challenger can still work if the incumbent's acquisition, like Nuance-Microsoft, creates openings for independent alternatives.
source: Fierce Healthcare coverage of Suki AI Series funding announcements

name: Corti
summary: Danish AI company builds real-time clinical decision support for emergency call centers and clinical documentation, helping dispatchers detect cardiac arrest and other emergencies faster, with growing deployments across European and US emergency services.
lesson: Applying AI decision support to a narrow, measurable emergency-response use case, such as faster cardiac arrest detection, creates an easily quantifiable ROI story for public-sector buyers.
source: Fierce Healthcare coverage of Corti's funding rounds

name: Freenome
summary: Built a multiomics, AI-powered blood test platform for early cancer detection, raising some of the largest funding rounds in AI diagnostics history and advancing a large-scale colorectal cancer screening trial toward FDA submission.
lesson: Multi-cancer early detection is a scientifically and commercially massive prize that can justify sustained mega-round fundraising even without near-term product revenue, as long as trial milestones keep landing.
source: Fierce Biotech coverage of Freenome funding round announcements

name: Exscientia
funding: Public via 2021 IPO; merged with Recursion 2024
summary: UK-founded AI drug design company was among the first to advance an AI-designed molecule into human clinical trials, went public on Nasdaq in 2021, and merged with Recursion Pharmaceuticals in 2024 to form a larger combined AI-biotech platform.
lesson: When two AI drug discovery platforms have complementary technology and overlapping pharma relationships, merging can create more scale and validation than either could achieve alone.
source: Recursion-Exscientia merger press release (2024)

name: Aitia (formerly GNS Healthcare)
summary: Long-running causal AI/simulation company, originally GNS Healthcare, rebranded as Aitia in 2021 and pivoted to building 'digital twin' simulations of cancer biology to find novel drug targets, continuing to operate with pharma and academic partnerships.
lesson: A rebrand paired with a sharpened technical narrative, from generic 'big data healthcare' to 'digital twins for oncology,' can extend the life of a long-running AI-health company.
source: Fierce Biotech coverage of Aitia's rebrand and funding (2021)

name: Deep 6 AI
summary: Uses natural language processing and AI to match patients to clinical trials in real time from unstructured EHR data, deployed across major health systems and academic medical centers to accelerate trial enrollment.
lesson: Solving a well-defined, high-cost pain point for pharma, such as slow clinical trial enrollment, with AI gives a clear, quantifiable ROI that justifies enterprise contracts.
source: Fierce Biotech coverage of Deep 6 AI funding rounds

name: BioXcel Therapeutics
summary: Used AI to identify new applications for existing drug compounds, successfully bringing an AI-informed drug (Igalmi, for acute agitation) through FDA approval in 2022, one of the few AI-driven drug discovery companies to reach approval and commercialization.
lesson: Applying AI to drug repurposing, finding new uses for known-safe compounds, can reach FDA approval faster and cheaper than discovering entirely novel molecules.
source: FDA approval announcement (2022) and BioXcel Therapeutics investor filings

name: HeartFlow
summary: Built an FDA-cleared AI platform (HeartFlow FFRct) that creates a personalized 3D model of coronary arteries from CT scans to noninvasively assess blockage severity, achieving broad payer coverage and hospital adoption over more than a decade.
lesson: Securing broad Medicare and commercial payer reimbursement codes for an AI diagnostic is often the single hardest and most valuable milestone to reach — and the one that ultimately determines durable success.
source: Fierce Biotech coverage of HeartFlow's reimbursement wins and funding

name: Cardiologs
summary: French AI company built an FDA-cleared deep-learning platform for automated ECG interpretation, and was acquired by Philips in 2022 to strengthen its cardiac diagnostics and patient monitoring portfolio.
lesson: A well-validated, narrow diagnostic AI algorithm, such as ECG interpretation, can become a highly attractive acquisition target for a large medical device incumbent looking to add AI capability quickly.
source: Philips acquisition press release (2022)

name: Regard (formerly HealthTensor)
summary: Builds an AI clinical decision support tool that automatically reviews a patient's full chart to surface diagnoses physicians may have missed and drafts clinical documentation, deployed across multiple health systems with strong physician adoption.
lesson: Combining diagnostic decision support with the administrative pain point of clinical documentation, rather than either alone, increases physician willingness to adopt AI at the point of care.
source: Fierce Healthcare coverage of Regard's Series B funding

name: Verge Genomics
summary: AI drug discovery company focused on neurodegenerative diseases such as ALS and Parkinson's, using human genomic data rather than animal models to identify novel drug targets, advancing its first AI-discovered ALS drug candidate into human clinical trials.
lesson: Focusing an AI drug discovery platform on a single high-unmet-need therapeutic area with a differentiated data source, such as human versus animal genomics, can produce clinical-stage credibility faster than a broad-platform approach.
source: Fierce Biotech coverage of Verge Genomics clinical trial announcements
